meta.name: IES-6 Impact of Event Scale-6
meta.description: The IES-6 is a brief 6-item self-report measure derived from the IES-R that assesses subjective distress caused by traumatic events. It captures the three core dimensions of post-traumatic stress (intrusion, avoidance, and hyperarousal) with just two items per dimension, making it suitable for screening purposes.
meta.author: Thoresen et al. (2010)
meta.reference: Thoresen S, Tambs K, Hussain A, Heir T, Johansen VA, Bisson JI. Brief measure of posttraumatic stress reactions: Impact of Event Scale-6. J Loss Trauma. 2010;15(3):238-247.
meta.instruction: Below is a list of difficulties people sometimes have after stressful life events. Please read each item and indicate how distressing each difficulty has been for you during the past 7 days.

questions.text: I thought about it when I didn’t mean to
I felt watchful or on guard
Other things kept making me think about it
I was aware that I still had a lot of feelings about it, but I didn’t deal with them
I tried not to think about it

ranges.label: Subclinical
ranges.description: Your score is within the subclinical range, suggesting that the stressful event has not caused significant ongoing psychological distress. This indicates that you are not currently showing significant post-traumatic stress reactions, and your psychological recovery is progressing well.
suggestions: Continue maintaining your current coping strategies
Allow yourself to process the experience at your own pace
Stay connected with supportive people in your life
Continue maintaining your good psychological health
Stay emotionally connected with friends and family

ranges.label: Mild Distress
ranges.description: Your score suggests mild post-traumatic distress. You may be experiencing some intrusive thoughts, avoidance behaviors, or heightened alertness. Mild trauma-related reactions are normal after experiencing stressful events and typically diminish over time.
suggestions: Monitor your symptoms over the coming weeks
Practice relaxation techniques such as deep breathing or mindfulness
Talk to someone you trust about your experiences
Maintain regular routines for sleep and daily activities
Allow yourself time and space to process your emotions

ranges.label: Moderate Distress
ranges.description: Your score indicates moderate post-traumatic distress. The event is having a notable impact on your well-being and may be interfering with daily functioning.
suggestions: Consider seeking support from a mental health professional
Practice grounding techniques when intrusive thoughts occur
Maintain a structured daily routine
Avoid isolating yourself; stay connected with others
Consider consulting a professional trained in trauma therapy

ranges.label: Severe Distress
ranges.description: Your score indicates severe post-traumatic distress that is likely significantly affecting your well-being and daily functioning. Your trauma-related reactions are quite pronounced and may be significantly impacting your functioning. Professional trauma-focused support is recommended.
suggestions: Please seek professional help from a trauma-specialized therapist
Evidence-based treatments such as EMDR or trauma-focused CBT can be very effective
If you are in crisis, contact a crisis helpline immediately
Create a safety plan for managing intense distress
Contact a trauma therapy specialist as soon as possible
